ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class; no specific entry for Teh Halia (ginger milk tea). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers hot tea beverages broadly; no specific Teh Halia or ginger milk tea code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Teh Halia. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: mixing with condensed milk and evaporated milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Teh Halia is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to 250 g, as commonly served at Malaysian and Singaporean kopitiams and mamak stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Teh Halia are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for teh tarik / ginger milk tea. The input record cites HPB as data_source with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the milk fat content of condensed and evaporated milk components using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture data are not reported in the HPB entry for this beverage.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Teh Halia. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with sweetened milk tea (GI approximately 50–60) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The fat and protein content of condensed and evaporated milk moderates the glycemic response relative to plain sugar water. Ginger has been reported in peer-reviewed literature to have modest glucose-modulating effects. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 28.0) / 100 = 15.4. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 11.2 × 2.5 = 28.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Caffeine content and moderate sugar load; sodium is low at 45 mg per 100 g (112.5 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Teh Halia to one serving per day due to caffeine stimulation of blood pressure, and should request reduced condensed milk to lower the sugar and caloric load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined sugar from condensed milk (10.5 g sugar per 100 g; approximately 26.3 g per 250 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should avoid standard Teh Halia and instead choose Teh O Halia Kosong (black ginger tea without sugar or milk) to eliminate the refined sugar and dairy fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from condensed and evaporated milk (estimated 1.1 g per 100 g) and high sugar content contributing to dyslipidaemia risk with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Teh Halia to occasional consumption and request reduced condensed milk, as the saturated fat and sugar content can adversely affect lipid profiles when consumed daily.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger (halia) contains gingerols with documented anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Teh Halia may offer mild symptomatic benefit for rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory gingerols in fresh ginger, but sugar intake should be monitored as excess sugar may promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh Halia is a popular hot ginger milk tea originating from Malaysia and Singapore, widely served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and mamak (Indian-Muslim) stalls. It is prepared by brewing strong black tea with freshly crushed ginger (halia in Malay), then combining the brew with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk. The mixture is 'pulled' — poured repeatedly between two vessels at height — to create a characteristic frothy top, in the same style as Teh Tarik. The result is a creamy, sweet, and spicy beverage with a warming ginger kick that complements the rich milky sweetness. Per 100 g, Teh Halia provides approximately 65 kcal, 1.2 g protein, 1.8 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrate (of which 10.5 g is sugar), with 45 mg sodium and 35 mg calcium. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 162.5 kcal and 26.3 g sugar, making it a moderate-calorie but high-sugar beverage. Clinically, the high refined sugar content from condensed milk makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes unless modified to a sugar-free and milk-free version (Teh O Halia Kosong). The caffeine content warrants caution for those with hypertension or caffeine sensitivity. The ginger component contributes gingerols, which have documented anti-inflammatory and mild antihypertensive properties. Teh Halia is considered a comforting and therapeutic drink in Malay and Mamak culinary culture, particularly during cool weather or illness.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Teh Halia?
Is Teh Halia good for diabetes?
What is the difference between Teh Tarik and Teh Halia?
Can I drink Teh Halia if I have high blood pressure?
What is ginger milk tea in Malay?
How much sugar is in Teh Halia?
What is Teh O Halia Kosong?
meal_context: comfort drink during illness or cold weather

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with canonical provenance and 0.95 confidence as reported in the input record. Saturated fat is estimated from milk component fatty acid profiles using USDA SR Legacy as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea, as no direct Teh Halia GI measurement is available in approved sources. Overall confidence is medium due to the GI estimation and saturated fat estimation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Teh Halia are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this traditional pulled ginger milk tea preparation as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g) is estimated from the known fatty acid composition of condensed milk and evaporated milk components per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01302, NDB 01085). All core macros (protein_g 1.2, fat_g 1.8, carbohydrate_g 11.2) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Teh Halia exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = 15.4 (moderate). The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are retained as contextually accurate for a single serving relative to daily reference intakes, but consumers should note the high sugar content.
